intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The AutoGen Code Execution Subsystem is the execution tier of a two-agent generator/executor pair: it receives LLM-produced code blocks (Python or shell), runs them in a configured environment, and returns stdout, stderr, and exit status to the calling agent. The remit authorizes five executors — LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor, DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor, DockerJupyterCodeExecutor, JupyterCodeExecutor, and AzureContainerCodeExecutor — with the containerized variants positioned as the production path and the Local variant explicitly requiring human approval for production use. Every executor must confine I/O to a configured work directory, enforce a wall-clock timeout, isolate the execution from the parent process's environment and credentials, and record each execution to an audit log carrying timestamp, executor type, source agent, exit status, and a digest of the code.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python library packaged in two namespaces: autogen_core.code_executor holds the CodeExecutor abstract base, CodeBlock / CodeResult dataclasses, and the user-defined-function machinery; autogen_ext.code_executors ships the five concrete executors in sibling subpackages (local/, docker/, docker_jupyter/, jupyter/, azure/) plus a shared _common.py with the file-name confinement helper get_file_name_from_content and a silence_pip regex transform. code_executors/__init__.py exposes a create_default_code_executor factory that selects Docker if available and falls back to Local otherwise. Each executor implements its own execute_code_blocks path — direct asyncio.create_subprocess_exec for Local, container.exec_run for Docker, nbclient.NotebookClient for Jupyter, a WebSocket kernel-gateway client for DockerJupyter, and an HTTP POST to code/execute for Azure — none of which share a common policy, approval, or audit layer.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is sandbox has the shape of isolation but not the substance, driven by a silent-downgrade chain rooted at code_executors/__init__.py:58-66 where create_default_code_executor wraps the Docker construction in a bare except Exception: pass and falls back to the Local executor on any failure, with only a warnings.warn as the operator signal. The Local executor it hands back claims in its docstring (local/__init__.py:57) to sanitize "dangerous commands" via regex match — no such sanitizer exists in the code path — and at local/__init__.py:397 the subprocess inherits the parent process's full os.environ.copy(), directly violating the remit's environment-isolation rule.

The Docker path is no safer than its config allows: containers are created at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550 with default Linux capabilities, no --cap-drop, no read-only rootfs, no --security-opt no-new-privileges, no resource limits, and the caller-supplied extra_volumes dict is merged straight into the volume mount table (line 546) — a one-line bypass of the remit's MUST-NOT-mount-host-volumes-without-approval clause. DockerJupyterServer additionally os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) at _jupyter_server.py:328 and ships the kernel-gateway token over plaintext ws:// (_jupyter_server.py:80, 124-125). Across all five executors there is zero structured audit logging of code-execution events; the few logging.* calls cover container lifecycle and pip-install info, not the per-execution timestamp / source / digest trail the remit mandates and that Monitor Continuously needs to score above Ad hoc.

rules.rule_text: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor — executes code directly on the host OS. Intended for trusted, isolated environments only.
No outbound network traffic from the executor itself (the executed code may make network calls if the sandbox permits, but the executor does not initiate its own).

rules.section: What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Confine all file read and write operations to a configured work directory; reject any path that resolves outside it.

rules.section: What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Enforce a configured wall-clock timeout on every code execution; terminate processes that exceed the timeout.

rules.section: What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Capture stdout, stderr, and exit status completely; do not silently discard output.

rules.section: What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Isolate the execution environment from the host caller: a failing or malicious execution must not be able to read or modify the parent process's environment variables, credentials, or state.

rules.section: What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Prevent persistence across executions except via the configured state mechanism. State never leaks between unrelated sessions.

rules.section: What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Record each execution to an audit log: timestamp, executor type, language, source (caller agent), work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.

rules.section: What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Execute code with host-level privileges (root, administrator, privileged Docker flags) when a less-privileged option is available for the same task.

rules.section: What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Accept or act on instructions embedded in the code source that attempt to escape the sandbox, escalate privileges, or exfiltrate data.

rules.section: What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Bypass the configured work-directory confinement under any condition, including symlinks, path traversal via .., absolute paths, or Docker volume mount overrides supplied at runtime.

rules.section: What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Load or execute code from remote URLs or unverified sources on behalf of the LLM; the LLM's generated code is the only accepted input.

rules.section: What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Silently swallow or transform error output before returning it to the caller.
LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor use in production. Default production deployments should use a containerized executor.
Mounting host volumes into the Docker executor at paths other than the configured work directory.

rules.section: What every executor must never do (docstring claim)
rules.rule_text: Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands in order to prevent self-destructive commands from being executed which may potentially affect the users environment.

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor silently downgrades from Docker to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor on any Docker failure, bypassing the approval requirement for local execution in production.
findings.description: The remit (R-14) requires human approval before LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor is used in production. The factory function turns that into a silent fallback whose only signal to the operator is a UserWarning that any well-behaved logging configuration filters by default. Combined with the absent sanitizer documented at local/__init__.py:57, an attacker who can perturb Docker availability (DOCKER_HOST env tampering, daemon kill, socket permission flip) flips the entire executor surface from container-isolated to host-OS execution without operator approval.
findings.policy_rule_text: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor use in production. Default production deployments should use a containerized executor.

evidence.snippet: if _is_docker_available() and DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor is not None: try: ... except Exception: pass — bare except swallows every Docker construction error and proceeds to the Local fallback at line 78 with only warnings.warn as the operator signal
Fallback to local if Docker fails to initialize — no approval gate, no error surface, no configuration switch to opt out of the silent downgrade
return LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor(work_dir=work_dir) — the production caller receives a host-OS executor and is expected to notice via a UserWarning that most logging configs suppress

recommended_actions: Remove the bare except Exception: pass at code_executors/__init__.py:64 and surface Docker construction errors to the caller; let the operator application decide whether to fall back

Replace the implicit fallback with an explicit allow_local_fallback: bool = False kwarg on create_default_code_executor; default-deny, and have it raise RuntimeError when Docker is unavailable and the flag is not set
When local fallback is allowed, log via logging.warning (not warnings.warn) so the event reaches centralized log pipelines, and include the original Docker exception in the message

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor passes the parent process's full os.environ to LLM-generated code, leaking every caller-side secret into the executed program.
findings.description: env = os.environ.copy() at local/__init__.py:397 means OPENAI_API_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN, database URLs, and every other secret the operator application loads into its own environment flow directly into the subprocess that runs the LLM's code. R-06 requires the execution to be unable to read the parent's environment variables, credentials, or state; this implementation is the literal opposite.
findings.policy_rule_text: Isolate the execution environment from the host caller: a failing or malicious execution must not be able to read or modify the parent process's environment variables, credentials, or state.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy() — the subprocess that runs the LLM's code inherits the entire parent environment; the only modification is prepending the virtualenv bin directory to PATH at line 400
asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(..., env=env) — the copied environment is passed verbatim; no allow-list filter, no secret-name denylist, no scrubbing of *_KEY / *_TOKEN / *_SECRET variables

recommended_actions: In _execute_code_dont_check_setup, replace env = os.environ.copy() with an explicit minimal env dict: PATH (rewritten to virtualenv bin if present), HOME (set to a per-execution work-dir-scoped path), LANG, and nothing else by default

Add an extra_env: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None constructor parameter so the operator can opt specific variables in by name; document this as the only supported way to expose caller-side state to executed code

Add a regression test under tests/code_executors/ that sets a sentinel env var (PRAXA_SECRET_CANARY) in the parent process, runs import os; print(os.environ.get('PRAXA_SECRET_CANARY')), and asserts the output is empty

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor accepts caller-supplied extra_volumes that are merged directly into the container mount table, bypassing work-directory confinement.
findings.description: The remit explicitly forbids bypassing work-directory confinement via "Docker volume mount overrides supplied at runtime" (R-11) and requires human approval for any host volume mount beyond the work directory (R-15). The executor exposes extra_volumes as an ordinary __init__ kwarg and merges it into the docker create call with a ** splat, with no validation, no allow-list, and no approval surface. A misconfigured operator application that passes a CLI flag from the LLM agent into the executor constructor turns the Docker sandbox into a chroot wrapper over arbitrary host paths.
findings.policy_rule_text: Bypass the configured work-directory confinement under any condition, including symlinks, path traversal via .., absolute paths, or Docker volume mount overrides supplied at runtime. / Mounting host volumes into the Docker executor at paths other than the configured work directory.

evidence.snippet: extra_volumes: Optional[Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]] = None — accepted as an ordinary constructor kwarg with no validation against the configured bind_dir

volumes={str(self.bind_dir.resolve()): {'bind': '/workspace', 'mode': 'rw'}, **self._extra_volumes} — the caller-supplied dict is splat-merged after the work directory, so it can shadow /workspace or mount /, /var/run/docker.sock, host SSH keys, etc.

Docstring example: extra_volumes = {'/home/user1/': {'bind': '/mnt/vol2', 'mode': 'rw'}, '/var/www': {'bind': '/mnt/vol1', 'mode': 'ro'}} — documents host-path mounts as the supported pattern with no security caveat

recommended_actions: Validate every entry of extra_volumes at constructor time: reject keys outside an operator-configured allowed_host_paths set, reject any path that resolves to /, /etc, /root, the Docker socket, or a parent of the work directory, and reject any entry whose mode is rw by default
Add an explicit require_volume_approval: bool = True flag; when set, any non-empty extra_volumes must be accompanied by an extra_volumes_approval_token that the operator application has signed

Update the docstring example at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:125 to remove host-path mounts and replace it with a named-volume example; add a security note that mounting host paths defeats the sandbox boundary

findings.summary: Work-directory confinement is enforced only on the # filename: header, not on file reads, writes, or subprocess CWD escapes during execution.
findings.description: get_file_name_from_content at _common.py:96-111 rejects a # filename: ../etc/passwd header by resolving the path and calling relative_to(workspace_path) — useful, but it is the only confinement check in the executor pipeline. Once the code runs, the subprocess inherits the work directory as its cwd, and any open('../foo', 'w'), os.chdir, shutil.copytree, or symlink-follow inside the executed code is unconstrained. R-03 calls for confining all file read and write operations to the work directory, not just the filename comment.
findings.policy_rule_text: Confine all file read and write operations to a configured work directory; reject any path that resolves outside it.

evidence.snippet: get_file_name_from_content only checks the '# filename:' header; it is the entire workspace-confinement implementation
cwd=self.work_dir is set on subprocess_exec but nothing constrains the subprocess from chdir/open above work_dir — Local executor runs against the full host filesystem
JupyterCodeExecutor runs nbclient against the parent Python process; the executed cell has unrestricted filesystem access regardless of output_dir

recommended_actions: For the Local executor, run the subprocess under firejail, bubblewrap, or an equivalent mount-namespace wrapper that hides everything outside the work directory; document that without one, R-03 cannot be enforced and Local is for ephemeral-host use only
For JupyterCodeExecutor, document explicitly in the class docstring that it does NOT enforce R-03 and is unsuitable for untrusted code; route operators to DockerJupyterCodeExecutor
Strengthen get_file_name_from_content to also reject any filename containing a symlink component once the work dir exists on disk

findings.summary: No execution-level audit log: none of the five executors records timestamp, source, code digest, or exit status of each execution, leaving the subsystem invisible to detection.
findings.description: R-08 mandates a per-execution audit record with timestamp, executor type, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest of the executed code. Across all five executors the only logging.* calls cover container lifecycle, pip-install info messages, and Docker stop / kill bookkeeping. There is no logging.info call at the entry to execute_code_blocks, no record of the code digest, and no per-execution event a SIEM could ingest. The remit's audit requirement is unaddressed at the layer Praxa can inspect.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record each execution to an audit log: timestamp, executor type, language, source (caller agent), work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.

evidence.snippet: execute_code_blocks entry point has no audit logging; the only logging calls in the file are pip-install info at 283 and temp-file deletion errors at 472
execute_code_blocks entry has no audit logging; logging calls in the file cover container lifecycle, not per-execution events
execute_code_blocks issues HTTP POSTs to Azure with zero local logging — the execution event leaves no trace in the host application's log stream at all
JupyterCodeExecutor.execute_code_blocks — no audit log calls; nbclient errors surface as Python tracebacks in the return value only

recommended_actions: Add a single _audit_log_execution(executor_type, language, source, work_dir, timeout, exit_code, code) helper to _common.py that emits a structured logging.info record with extra={'event': 'code_execution', ...} and a sha256 digest of code (not the body)

Call the helper from every execute_code_blocks entry point — Local (line 324), Docker (line 405), Jupyter (line 162), DockerJupyter (line 197), Azure (line 393) — with the appropriate executor_type label
Document the audit-log schema in autogen_core/code_executor/_base.py next to the abstract method so subclasses inherit the obligation
Add a regression test that captures the structured log record and asserts every required field is present

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor docstring claims dangerous-command regex sanitization that does not exist in the code path.
findings.description: The class docstring at local/__init__.py:57 states: "Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands in order to prevent self-destructive commands from being executed which may potentially affect the users environment." No such sanitizer is invoked in _execute_code_dont_check_setup — the only pre-execution transform is silence_pip, which adds -qqq to pip install lines. This is a documented-but-not-implemented control: an operator who reads the docstring during a security review will believe a denylist is active when none is.
findings.policy_rule_text: Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands in order to prevent self-destructive commands from being executed which may potentially affect the users environment.

evidence.snippet: Docstring asserts regex sanitization against a list of dangerous commands — the claim is part of the public class doc that AutoGen users rely on for security posture

_execute_code_dont_check_setup body — only pre-execution code transform is silence_pip(code, lang) at line 357; no dangerous-command match, no denylist, no early-exit on rm -rf, sudo, curl|sh, dd of=, etc.
silence_pip is the only sanitize-shaped helper in _common.py; its scope is adding -qqq to pip install, not blocking destructive commands

recommended_actions: Remove the false claim from the docstring at local/__init__.py:57 immediately — the documented-but-absent control is worse than no documentation, because it gives reviewers a false negative

If a denylist is the intended design, add a minimal regex denylist (rm\s+-rf\s+/, :\(\)\{\s*:\|:&\s*\};:, dd\s+if=.*of=/dev/, mkfs, curl\s+.*\|\s*(bash|sh)) and wire it into _execute_code_dont_check_setup at the start of the per-block loop with a clear exit-code-1 short-circuit
Document explicitly that any denylist is best-effort and the only real isolation is at the OS / container layer

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with default capabilities and no security hardening — no cap_drop, no read-only rootfs, no no-new-privileges, no resource limits.
findings.description: R-09 forbids host-level privileges when a less-privileged option is available. The containers.create call at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550 omits every hardening primitive Docker offers: no cap_drop=['ALL'], no security_opt=['no-new-privileges:true'], no read_only=True rootfs, no user='nobody' or non-root UID, no pids_limit, no mem_limit, no cpu_quota, no network_mode='none'. Container escapes via kernel-vulnerability, fork-bomb resource exhaustion, and network exfiltration are all in scope by default.
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute code with host-level privileges (root, administrator, privileged Docker flags) when a less-privileged option is available for the same task.

evidence.snippet: client.containers.create(self._image, name=..., entrypoint='/bin/sh', tty=True, detach=True, auto_remove=..., volumes=..., working_dir='/workspace', extra_hosts=..., device_requests=...) — no cap_drop, no security_opt, no read_only, no user, no resource limits in the kwargs
Default image is python:3-slim which runs as root by default; no override is set in the executor

recommended_actions: Add the following defaults to the containers.create call at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537: cap_drop=['ALL'], security_opt=['no-new-privileges:true'], read_only=True (with a tmpfs at /tmp), user='nobody:nogroup' (or a non-root UID baked into the image), pids_limit=256, mem_limit='512m', network_mode='none'
Expose each as an opt-out constructor kwarg with a hardened default; document the security implications of opting out in the docstring
Add a regression test that spawns the executor and asserts container.attrs['HostConfig'] shows the hardened settings

findings.summary: DockerJupyterServer makes the bind directory world-writable (chmod 0o777) and ships its kernel-gateway token over plaintext ws://.
findings.description: Two defects in one file. (1) os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) at _jupyter_server.py:328 makes the host-side bind directory world-writable for every UID on the host — any local process can plant code in the executor's workspace. (2) _get_ws_base_url at _jupyter_server.py:80 hardcodes ws:// and get_kernel_client at _jupyter_server.py:124-125 uses that URL for the kernel gateway WebSocket while including the auth token in the Authorization header — the token traverses an unencrypted channel even when the deployment is non-local. Both defects defeat the isolation the Docker container nominally provides.
findings.policy_rule_text: Isolate the execution environment from the host caller: a failing or malicious execution must not be able to read or modify the parent process's environment variables, credentials, or state. / Bypass the configured work-directory confinement under any condition, including symlinks, path traversal via .., absolute paths, or Docker volume mount overrides supplied at runtime.

evidence.snippet: os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) — the host directory bound into the container is opened to every UID on the host with no comment explaining why
_get_ws_base_url returns f'ws://{host}{port}' unconditionally — there is no use_https-equivalent toggle for the WebSocket channel
ws = await websockets.connect(ws_url, additional_headers=self._get_headers()) — auth token in headers, transport unencrypted; vulnerable to passive sniffing on any non-loopback link

recommended_actions: Replace os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) at _jupyter_server.py:328 with a precise UID/GID grant that matches the container's NB_UID/NB_GID; document the mode used and why it is sufficient

Add a use_https field to JupyterConnectionInfo (it already has use_https for the REST URL — wire it through to _get_ws_base_url so it returns wss:// when set); reject plaintext ws:// for non-loopback hosts in JupyterClient.__init__
Default the generated token's transport to wss:// with a self-signed cert when no certificate is configured; treat unencrypted-token transport over a non-loopback link as a hard error

findings.summary: Stdout and stderr are concatenated into a single string in three of five executors, making it impossible for the caller to distinguish failure traces from program output.
findings.description: R-05 requires complete capture of stdout, stderr, and exit status; R-13 forbids silently swallowing or transforming error output. The Local executor concatenates stderr.decode() + stdout.decode() into logs_all (local/__init__.py:458-459); the Docker executor returns the container's exec_run output decoded as a single string (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:303) — its exec_run call defaults to stream=False, demux=False, so stderr is interleaved with stdout; the Azure executor concatenates data.get('stderr','') + data.get('stdout','') at azure/_azure_container_code_executor.py:475. Downstream LLM reasoning cannot reliably tell "the program printed an error message" apart from "the program crashed", and a sufficiently structured stderr can poison the next prompt.
findings.policy_rule_text: Capture stdout, stderr, and exit status completely; do not silently discard output. / Silently swallow or transform error output before returning it to the caller.

evidence.snippet: logs_all += stderr.decode(); logs_all += stdout.decode() — both streams are flattened into a single field with no per-stream label
output = result.output.decode('utf-8') — docker.exec_run with default demux=False returns interleaved stdout/stderr
logs_all += data.get('stderr', '') + data.get('stdout', '') — Azure response provides both streams; the executor flattens them

recommended_actions: Extend CodeResult in autogen_core/code_executor/_base.py with a stderr: str field (and a deprecation shim that leaves output unchanged for one minor version)

Update each executor's _execute_code_dont_check_setup to populate stderr separately: Local uses stderr.decode() directly; Docker passes demux=True to exec_run; Azure reads the stderr field from the response JSON; Jupyter / DockerJupyter map the kernel error message type to stderr
Document for downstream consumers that any stderr fragment used for LLM context must be tagged as untrusted content per R-13

findings.summary: JupyterCodeExecutor runs against the host Python with no isolation from the parent process — state leaks between sessions if a single executor instance is reused.
findings.description: R-07 requires that state never leak between unrelated sessions. JupyterCodeExecutor at jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py:48-309 instantiates an nbclient.NotebookClient with the named kernelspec (default python3) on the host. Every cell executes in the same kernel and the kernel sees the same module namespace, file system, and environment as the operator application that imported it. Two consecutive callers of the same JupyterCodeExecutor instance share variables, imported modules, and any side-effects on the host. Only restart() recycles the kernel; the executor itself does not enforce restart between unrelated sessions.
findings.policy_rule_text: Prevent persistence across executions except via the configured state mechanism. State never leaks between unrelated sessions.

evidence.snippet: NotebookClient(nb=notebook, kernel_name=self._kernel_name, timeout=self._timeout, allow_errors=True) — kernel runs in the same OS process tree as the host application; nothing scopes the kernel to a session identifier

Class docstring includes a danger note: 'This will execute code on the local machine. If being used with LLM generated code, caution should be used.' — accurate but does not address the session-leakage shape of the risk

recommended_actions: Document explicitly in the JupyterCodeExecutor class docstring that the executor MUST be instantiated per-session and that restart() is required between unrelated callers
Add a _session_id field to JupyterCodeExecutor and reject calls to execute_code_blocks whose cancellation_token originates from a different session than the previous one
Recommend operators use DockerJupyterCodeExecutor instead for any multi-tenant deployment

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor pulls images by tag with no digest pinning or signature verification, exposing the runtime to tag-swap supply-chain attacks.
findings.description: The Docker executor defaults to image='python:3-slim' (a floating tag) and at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:524 pulls the tag if not present locally with client.images.pull. There is no digest pin, no Cosign / Notary signature verification, and no checksum gate. An attacker who can compromise the operator's registry credentials, pollute a mirror, or tag-squat in the registry can swap the image between scans. Pinning by digest (python@sha256:...) is the minimum bar.
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute code with host-level privileges (root, administrator, privileged Docker flags) when a less-privileged option is available for the same task.

evidence.snippet: image: str = 'python:3-slim' — default config uses a floating tag, not a digest
await asyncio.to_thread(client.images.pull, self._image) — no digest verification, no signature check, no checksum gate before run

recommended_actions: Change the default image to a digest-pinned reference (e.g., python@sha256:<current-digest>) and document the bump cadence
Add an optional image_digest: Optional[str] = None kwarg; when set, after pull, verify client.images.get(self._image).id == image_digest and raise if it differs
Document the supply-chain implication in the class docstring next to the existing usage example

findings.summary: No adversarial-testing artifacts present in the staged workspace; sandbox-escape, env-leak, and volume-bypass scenarios are not covered by colocated tests.
findings.description: The staged workspace (the two code_executor packages) contains no test files, no fuzzing harness, and no adversarial fixture. Tests for the executors exist elsewhere in the upstream repository under python/packages/autogen-ext/tests/code_executors/, but they are functional unit tests of the happy path — they do not target the specific sandbox-escape, env-leak, and volume-bypass scenarios the remit's MUST-NOTs imply. The Build-an-AI-Red-Team category cannot rise above Ad hoc without evidence that adversarial testing of these surfaces drove design changes.
findings.policy_rule_text: Isolate the execution environment from the host caller: a failing or malicious execution must not be able to read or modify the parent process's environment variables, credentials, or state. / Bypass the configured work-directory confinement under any condition, including symlinks, path traversal via .., absolute paths, or Docker volume mount overrides supplied at runtime.

evidence.snippet: find workspace -type f returns 15 implementation files and zero test files; no adversarial fixtures, no fuzzing harness, no sandbox-escape regression suite colocated with the executors

recommended_actions: Add an adversarial/ test suite under python/packages/autogen-ext/tests/code_executors/ with at least: env-leak canary (parent env var must not appear in subprocess output), extra_volumes denial test, path-traversal-in-filename test, dangerous-command denylist test (once implemented), and stderr/stdout separation test
Wire the suite into the CI matrix and require it to pass on every PR that touches code_executors/
Document in the executor docstrings that any new executor implementation MUST add fixtures to this suite before it ships

positives.title: Work-directory filename confinement on the # filename: header
positives.description: get_file_name_from_content resolves the filename comment relative to the work directory and calls Path.relative_to(workspace_path.resolve()), raising on any path that escapes the work directory. It is narrow (only the filename comment), but where it applies it is correct and consistently invoked by Local and Docker executors.

positives.title: Wall-clock timeout enforced on every execution path
positives.description: Every executor honors a timeout constructor parameter and applies it: Local via asyncio.wait_for (local/__init__.py:441), Docker via the in-container timeout command and asyncio.wait_for (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:360), Jupyter via asyncio.wait_for on the cell execute task (jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py:207), Azure via aiohttp ClientTimeout (azure/_azure_container_code_executor.py:435).

positives.title: Explicit UserWarning on LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor construction
positives.description: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor.__init__ emits a UserWarning recommending DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor instead. This does not satisfy the remit's R-14 approval requirement (warnings are not approval), but it is a real operator-facing signal and worth crediting.

positives.title: DockerJupyterServer auto-generates a 32-byte hex token when none is supplied
positives.description: When no token is passed, DockerJupyterServer generates a 64-character hex token via secrets.token_hex(32) rather than running token-less; auth is on by default. The plaintext-ws:// finding (PRAX-2026-05-11-008) erodes the value, but the credential-strength default itself is correct.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in the staged workspace and no executor writes a structured execution log at runtime — the absence is itself the finding PRAX-2026-05-11-005 against R-08.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The subsystem has correct individual primitives — timeouts on every path, filename confinement in _common.py, an explicit UserWarning before Local is used, a hex token by default for Jupyter — but the controls that the remit actually depends on (parent-environment isolation, host-volume-mount discipline, container hardening, audit logging, error-stream separation) are either absent or are present in name only. The single category dragging the weighted score down is Implement Zero Trust at 1; the silent Docker-to-Local downgrade and the os.environ.copy leak are each a Critical finding on their own and together describe a sandbox that an attacker can demote from the operator side without ever touching the LLM code path.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The executor's domain — accept a code block, run it, return stdout/stderr/exit — is narrow and consistently enforced; SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES is a small allow-list per executor (local/__init__.py:130-138, docker/_docker_code_executor.py:140-148) and unrecognized languages short-circuit with exit=1. The remit's named counterparties (calling agent, operator application, containerization layer) are all single-call synchronous boundaries, not channels the executor can expand into.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The executor itself ingests no external content beyond the code block it is handed and the optional packages list for FunctionWithRequirements; data discipline at this layer is minimal but reasonable. The drag is that executor stdout and stderr are merged and returned verbatim to the calling agent (PRAX-2026-05-11-009), so the agent's next prompt can be poisoned by program output the executor did not tag as untrusted.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The remit's hard isolation rules (R-03, R-06, R-09, R-11) are each unmet at code level: os.environ.copy() leaks parent secrets (PRAX-2026-05-11-002), extra_volumes bypasses work-directory confinement (PRAX-2026-05-11-003), the Docker container runs with default capabilities (PRAX-2026-05-11-007), and the documented dangerous-command sanitizer does not exist (PRAX-2026-05-11-006). The timeout and filename-confinement primitives that do exist keep this above Absent.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Python dependencies are declared in the upstream package metadata and the docker extra is gated by an import-guard try/except (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:43-51); the default Docker image is a floating tag (python:3-slim) with no digest pin or signature check (PRAX-2026-05-11-011), and the DockerJupyterServer image is built from an inline Dockerfile (_jupyter_server.py:267-287) that pulls from quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation with no integrity verification.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Functional tests exist upstream at python/packages/autogen-ext/tests/code_executors/ (not in the staged workspace), but no adversarial fixture targeting the sandbox-escape, env-leak, or volume-bypass scenarios that the remit's MUST-NOT clauses imply (PRAX-2026-05-11-012). No evidence of red-team-driven design changes.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No execute_code_blocks entry point in any of the five executors emits an audit-log record (PRAX-2026-05-11-005). The few logging.* calls present cover container start/stop and pip-install info, not the per-execution timestamp / source / digest / exit-status trail R-08 mandates. KB inference rule fires: no execution logging caps Monitor at 1.
